source: VS Partners / Financial Times Global Seattle Symposium — Global technology investment: hype, hope, reality
summary: 5G is the most overhyped technology trend — it enables no new applications unlike 3G or 4G
summary_extended: Baker argues that 5G is the most overhyped technology trend of the current cycle, contrasting it sharply with 3G and 4G, which enabled genuinely transformative new applications — 3G unlocked the mobile internet and 4G enabled streaming services like Spotify and Netflix. He contends that 5G enables no fundamentally new applications: IoT, the most commonly cited use case, does not require 5G beyond what 4G already provides. As a result, the massive capex being spent by telecom operators and handset manufacturers is purely defensive — necessary to remain competitive but incapable of generating new revenue streams or growth. Baker frames this as a consensus-defying view held against generalist investors and journalists who have uncritically accepted the 5G hype narrative, and implies the investment implication is to be skeptical of 5G-themed investment theses and the growth premiums attached to companies marketed as 5G beneficiaries.
detail: Baker argues 3G enabled the mobile internet and 4G enabled streaming (Spotify, Netflix), but 5G does not enable any fundamentally new applications. IoT does not require 5G beyond what 4G already provides. Telecom operators and handset makers must spend the capex just to stay competitive — it is defensive, not growth-generating.
confidence_evidence: Baker states this as a firm personal view, uses the phrase 'extremely overhyped,' and contrasts it with prior generations with specific examples. Frames it as a consensus-defying view vs. generalists and journalists.
quote_fragment: 5g just does not enable anything new and foundational
